attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with a slowly worsening deformity in his right hand. There is no history of any trauma. He has a seven year history of type 2 diabetes, for which he uses glibenclamide. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history and drinks about three cans of beer a day.

On examination there is a thickened cord on the palmar aspect of the right hand, with the ring finger being held in a semi-flexed position (Image). He has difficulty extending his ring finger fully, due to contracture at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a Dupuytren's contracture affecting his right ring finger. This condition is characterised by the thickening and contracture of the palmar fascia, resulting in the formation of nodules and cords in the hands. Dupuytren's contracture typically affects the ring and little fingers, causing difficulty in extending these fingers fully and resulting in contractures at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. This results in painless deformity of the ring and little fingers. Several factors can contribute to the development of Dupuytren's contracture, including genetic predisposition, smoking, alcohol consumption, and diabetes mellitus.

De Quervain's tenosynovitis is characterised by inflammation of the tendons and their sheaths at the base of the thumb. It typically presents with pain, swelling, and difficulty with thumb movement.

Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic inflammatory disorder that primarily affects the joints symmetrically. It typically involves the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints and the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints of digits. The distal interphalangeal joints are usually spared. The deformities seen in rheumatoid arthritis may include ulnar drift (deviation of fingers towards the ulnar side), swan-neck deformity (hyperextension of the proximal interphalangeal joint with flexion of the distal interphalangeal joint), and Boutonnière deformity (hyperflexion of the PIP joint and hyperextension of the DIP joint). Subcutaneous nodules may appear over the dorsal aspect of IP and MCP joints.

Trigger finger or stenosing tenosynovitis is characterised by the inflammation and narrowing of the sheath that surrounds the tendons in the fingers, leading to difficulty in extending or flexing the affected finger smoothly. The trigger finger commonly affects the thumb and the ring finger, however, it can affect any finger. It does not have thickening of the palmar fascia - as is shown in the photograph.

Ulnar claw hand results from ulnar nerve compression or damage and is characterised by weakness and atrophy of the intrinsic hand muscles, leading to hyperextension of the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion of the interphalangeal joints of the ring and little fingers. While some similarities exist in the presentation (involvement of the ring and little fingers), the ulnar claw hand typically does not involve thickened cords and nodules in the palmar fascia, as seen in this case.
learningPoints: Dupuytren's contracture is characterized by the thickening and contracture of the palmar fascia, resulting in the formation of nodules and cords, typically leading to progressive flexion contractures of the ring and little fingers.
references: Walthall J, Anand P, Rehman UH. Dupuytren Contracture. [Updated 2023 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526074/

Mohammed RH, Bhutta BS. Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis. [Updated 2023 Aug 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560890/
